Key Families / People: Sais, Lepine, Birston, Cardinal, Parisien, Conway, Roussin, Marcoux, Laramee
Founding Story & Driver: A "shanty town" in south Winnipeg inhabited by Métis families between 1901 and 1959. Families lived here after being pushed out of the city center.
Modern Impact & Status: The community was forcibly evicted in 1959 to make way for Grant Park Shopping Centre and School. A commemorative "Kettle" monument stands near Beaumont Station.

Key Families / People: Families of the RM of Ellice-Archie
Founding Story & Driver: A thriving Métis community until 1938, when it was designated as a community pasture under the Prairie Farm Rehabilitation Act. Homes and the church were burned to force dispersal.
Modern Impact & Status: The site is now a community pasture, but the cemetery remains a sacred site of pilgrimage for Red River Métis citizens.

Key Families / People: Jerome, Langan, Lucier, Ledoux, Wheeler, Larocque
Founding Story & Driver: Located north of Roblin. Families settled here to escape government intervention, but many were later displaced by the creation of community pastures in the late 1930s.
Modern Impact & Status: These remain vibrant communities with a strong Michif linguistic heritage. The San Clara Parish Cemetery is a key historical landmark.

Key Families / People: Campbell, Chaboyer, Chartrand, Ducharme, Nabess, Azure, Lambert, Bourgoise, Lagimodiere
Founding Story & Driver: A productive Métis community on the Saskatchewan River dating back to 1874. It was self-sufficient with its own school, church, and fur trading post.
Modern Impact & Status: The community was destroyed in the 1960s by flooding from the Grand Rapids Dam, which wiped out the local trapping and fishing economy.

Key Families / People: Father Belcourt, Angelique Nolin, Marguerite Nolin
Founding Story & Driver: Established in 1832 as a mission on the Assiniboine River to support the Métis plains hunters and wintering site populations.
Modern Impact & Status: Historically significant as the location of the first Métis school in the area. Now part of the St. Eustache region.

Founding Story & Driver: A road allowance community located 7km northwest of The Pas. By 1959, it housed over 125 Métis citizens living on Crown Land.
Modern Impact & Status: Now integrated with the Opaskwayak Cree Nation (OCN) land, but remembered for its specific Métis history.

Key Families / People: George Boucher, Joe Venne, Pete Ducharme, William Smith
Founding Story & Driver: A sister community to Rooster Town in Winnipeg, located south of Fort Rouge. Named for the metal shanties built by residents.
Modern Impact & Status: Completely dismantled during the mid-20th century urban expansion of Winnipeg.

Founding Story & Driver: Established recently in 1988 following the displacement of Métis citizens from the Bloodvein Indian Reserve.
Modern Impact & Status: A contemporary example of ongoing Métis displacement and the resilience of road allowance communities.
